spans.content: Towards Controllable Image Generation through
Representation-Conditioned Diffusion Models
Nithesh Chandher Karthikeyan∗, Jonas Unger∗, and Gabriel Eilertsen∗
∗Linkoping University, Norrk ¨ oping, Sweden ¨
Email: {nithesh.chandher.karthikeyan, jonas.unger, gabriel.eilertsen}@liu.se
Abstract—Diffusion models have emerged as powerful
tools for high-quality image generation and editing, but
guiding these models to produce specific outputs remains
a challenge. Conventional approaches rely on conditioning
mechanisms, such as text prompts or semantic maps, which
require extensively annotated datasets. In this preliminary
work, we explore diffusion models conditioned on represen-
tations from a pre-trained self-supervised model. The self-
conditioning mechanism not only improves the quality of
unconditional image generation, but also provides a repre-
sentation space that can be used to control the generation.
We explore this conditioning space by identifying directions
of variations, and demonstrate promising properties in terms
of smoothness and disentanglement.
I. INTRODUCTION
Diffusion models have transformed the field of genera-
tive modeling, offering a powerful alternative to previous
models, such as Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
[1], for high-quality image synthesis and editing. How-
ever, GANs provide a well-structured and disentangled
latent space, facilitating precise modifications of fine-
grained image details while preserving unrelated attributes
[2], [3], [4]. In contrast, formulating a comparable latent
space in diffusion models remains a challenging research
problem. Over the years, various approaches have been
explored to address this challenge, including inversion
into the text-conditioning space [5], manipulation of acti-
vations of the denoising model [6], [7], and joint learning
of a semantically meaningful latent space [8].
Recently, representation-conditioning has been demon-
strated for the purpose of improving the quality of uncon-
ditional generation, by providing guidance through self-
supervision from extracted image representations [9]. We
see this as a promising direction not only for improving
unconditional generation but also for providing a latent
space that can be used for controllable generation. How-
ever, to our knowledge, representation-conditioned diffu-
sion models have not previously been thoroughly tested
for this purpose. Thus, in this paper we present initial
experiments on controlling diffusion models through a
pre-trained image representation space.
II. RELATED WORK
Text Conditioning: Conventional methods for con-
ditioning diffusion models involve using text prompts,
bounding boxes, or semantic maps as input [10]. Textual
Inversion [11] maps a semantic or concept from a few
images to a unique word token, whereas DreamBooth [12]
achieves a similar goal by fine-tuning the Imagen model

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Image Distribution"] --> B["Pre-Trained DINO"]
    B --> C["Representation Distribution"]
    D["Noise Distribution"] --> E["Representation Distribution"]
    E --> F["Denoising U-Net"]
    F --> G["D"]
    G --> H["Image Distribution"]
    I["Trainable"] --> J["Frozen"]
    K["Image"] --> L["Frozen"]
```
Fig. 1. The Representation Conditioned Diffusion Model consists of
two parts: (a) a pre-trained self-supervised encoder (DINO in our case)
maps the image distribution to a representation distribution, and (b) a
denoising U-Net maps a noise distribution to the image distribution,
conditioned on the representation distribution from (a).
with a few reference images. Custom Diffusion [13] learns
multiple concepts by fine-tuning the cross-attention blocks
in the diffusion model. However, these methods are highly
dependent on effective prompt engineering, which can
be limiting when achieving precise control over intricate
details and may struggle to adapt to unstructured datasets.
H-Space Manipulations: Kwon et al. [6] identifies
a latent space based on activations of the denoising
model over various timesteps. This semantic latent space,
known as H-space, is utilized to discover interpretable
and disentangled directions [7]. In contrast, our goal is to
establish a well-structured latent space that enables direct
sampling and controlled generation.
Representation Conditioning: Early research on Rep-
resentation Conditioned Diffusion Models (RCDM) in-
vestigate how much information from the input image
is preserved in the generated output when conditioned
on its representation [14]. Representation Conditioned
Generation (RCG) [9] uses representations from a self-
supervised model to condition an image generator. How-
ever, it introduces an additional layer by training a
representation generator for conditioning, enabling fully
unsupervised image generation. Diffusion Autoencoders
(Diffusion AEs) [8] employ an explicit semantic encoder
that learns some representation from the training images,
alongside a conditional DDIM that acts both as a stochas-
tic encoder and decoder. However, unlike RCG, Diffusion
AEs do not provide the same quality improvement in
unconditional generation.
Towards Controllable Image Generation through
Representation-Conditioned Diffusion Models
Nithesh Chandher Karthikeyan∗, Jonas Unger∗, and Gabriel Eilertsen∗
∗Linkoping University, Norrk ¨ oping, Sweden ¨
Email: {nithesh.chandher.karthikeyan, jonas.unger, gabriel.eilertsen}@liu.se
Abstract—Diffusion models have emerged as powerful
tools for high-quality image generation and editing, but
guiding these models to produce specific outputs remains
a challenge. Conventional approaches rely on conditioning
mechanisms, such as text prompts or semantic maps, which
require extensively annotated datasets. In this preliminary
work, we explore diffusion models conditioned on represen-
tations from a pre-trained self-supervised model. The self-
conditioning mechanism not only improves the quality of
unconditional image generation, but also provides a repre-
sentation space that can be used to control the generation.
We explore this conditioning space by identifying directions
of variations, and demonstrate promising properties in terms
of smoothness and disentanglement.
I. INTRODUCTION
Diffusion models have transformed the field of genera-
tive modeling, offering a powerful alternative to previous
models, such as Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
[1], for high-quality image synthesis and editing. How-
ever, GANs provide a well-structured and disentangled
latent space, facilitating precise modifications of fine-
grained image details while preserving unrelated attributes
[2], [3], [4]. In contrast, formulating a comparable latent
space in diffusion models remains a challenging research
problem. Over the years, various approaches have been
explored to address this challenge, including inversion
into the text-conditioning space [5], manipulation of acti-
vations of the denoising model [6], [7], and joint learning
of a semantically meaningful latent space [8].
Recently, representation-conditioning has been demon-
strated for the purpose of improving the quality of uncon-
ditional generation, by providing guidance through self-
supervision from extracted image representations [9]. We
see this as a promising direction not only for improving
unconditional generation but also for providing a latent
space that can be used for controllable generation. How-
ever, to our knowledge, representation-conditioned diffu-
sion models have not previously been thoroughly tested
for this purpose. Thus, in this paper we present initial
experiments on controlling diffusion models through a
pre-trained image representation space.
II. RELATED WORK
Text Conditioning: Conventional methods for con-
ditioning diffusion models involve using text prompts,
bounding boxes, or semantic maps as input [10]. Textual
Inversion [11] maps a semantic or concept from a few
images to a unique word token, whereas DreamBooth [12]
achieves a similar goal by fine-tuning the Imagen model
with a few reference images. Custom Diffusion [13] learns
multiple concepts by fine-tuning the cross-attention blocks
in the diffusion model. However, these methods are highly
dependent on effective prompt engineering, which can
be limiting when achieving precise control over intricate
details and may struggle to adapt to unstructured datasets.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Image Distribution"] --> B["Pre-Trained DINO"]
    B --> C["Representation Distribution"]
    D["Noise Distribution"] --> E["Representation Distribution"]
    E --> F["Denoising U-Net"]
    F --> G["D"]
    G --> H["Image Distribution"]
    I["Trainable"] --> J["Frozen"]
    K["Image"] --> L["Frozen"]
```
Fig. 1. The Representation Conditioned Diffusion Model consists of
two parts: (a) a pre-trained self-supervised encoder (DINO in our case)
maps the image distribution to a representation distribution, and (b) a
denoising U-Net maps a noise distribution to the image distribution,
conditioned on the representation distribution from (a).
H-Space Manipulations: Kwon et al. [6] identifies
a latent space based on activations of the denoising
model over various timesteps. This semantic latent space,
known as H-space, is utilized to discover interpretable
and disentangled directions [7]. In contrast, our goal is to
establish a well-structured latent space that enables direct
sampling and controlled generation.
Representation Conditioning: Early research on Rep-
resentation Conditioned Diffusion Models (RCDM) in-
vestigate how much information from the input image
is preserved in the generated output when conditioned
on its representation [14]. Representation Conditioned
Generation (RCG) [9] uses representations from a self-
supervised model to condition an image generator. How-
ever, it introduces an additional layer by training a
representation generator for conditioning, enabling fully
unsupervised image generation. Diffusion Autoencoders
(Diffusion AEs) [8] employ an explicit semantic encoder
that learns some representation from the training images,
alongside a conditional DDIM that acts both as a stochas-
tic encoder and decoder. However, unlike RCG, Diffusion
AEs do not provide the same quality improvement in
unconditional generation.

spans.content: III. METHOD
Training a Representation Conditioned Diffusion
Model (RCDM) can be broken down into two stages:
First, images are mapped to a lower-dimensional space us-
ing a pre-trained Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) encoder.
Then, a diffusion model is trained while conditioning on
these representations (see Figure 1). In this work, we
adapt the method from [9], which uses the MoCo v3
encoder and the MAGE generator, to generation through
the latent diffusion model (LDM) [10] conditioned on
DINO representations [15]. That is, denoising in the LDM
latent space is guided by the information provided by an
image’s DINO representation. While it is possible to train
a separate representation generator for fully unconditioned
generation, in this work we perform experiments on
representations from a separate test dataset.
A. Data and Training Setup
For the experiments, two datasets were used: (a) LSUN
Churches [16] and (b) Celeb-A [17]. The pre-trained
DINO encoder [15] extracts 768-dimensional represen-
tations from these datasets. For image compression in
LDM, a pre-trained VAE model with KL-divergence loss
[18] was used, and RCDMs were trained for each dataset
using a conditional de-noising U-Net.
IV. PROPERTIES OF THE REPRESENTATION SPACE
In this section, we perform experiments to validate
the effectiveness of using the representation space for
controllable image generation.
A. Perturbation and Interpolation
Given a representation vector C, we sample a noise
vector
\epsilon \sim \mathcal { N } ( 0 , 1 )
and perturb the representation vector
according to
\hat { \mathcal { C } } = \mathcal { C } + \lambda \epsilon .
, where λ controls the perturbation
strength. Figure 3 shows the generated images at different
perturbation strengths for two methods: Diffusion Inver-
sion [5], trained on the STL-10 dataset [19] and RCDM,
trained on LSUN-Churches [16]. At higher noise levels
( \lambda > 0 . 4 )
, Diffusion Inversion exhibits significant quality
degradation, while RCDM modifies image content while
maintaining image quality and consistency.
We can also perform interpolations in the representation
space. Given two representation vectors
\mathcal { C } _ { 1 }
and
\mathcal { C } _ { 2 } .
, a new
vector can be obtained by
\hat { \mathcal { C } } = \alpha \mathcal { C } _ { 1 } + ( 1 - \alpha ) \mathcal { C } _ { 2 }
. The
resulting image generated from
\hat { \mathcal { C } }
will exhibit features of

spans.html:
MethodsReference Imageλ
0.00.10.20.40.6
Diffusion Inversion
RCDM

spans.content: Fig. 3. Images generated by perturbing the embedding vector
\hat { \mathcal { C } }
(from
the reference image) using Gaussian noise with varying strength λ.
both input images, with the influence of each determined
by the interpolation factor α. Figure 2 shows the images
generated at different interpolation strengths for three
methods. In Stable Diffusion [10], two text embeddings
are extracted from separate text prompts using CLIP, and
the generated images represent interpolations between
these embeddings. For Diffusion Inversion and RCDM,
images are produced by interpolating between two learned
embeddings or representations (in the case of RCDM).
The results indicate that RCDM provides a semantically
smoother transition between reference images. In Diffu-
sion Inversion the images become indistinguishable in the
middle, while for Stable Diffusion a sudden transition
between images is observed for α values 0.5-0.6.
B. Semantic Directions
a) Supervised Semantic Directions: In a supervised
setting with access to image annotations, identifying se-
mantic directions in the representation space of RCDM
becomes more direct. Figure 4 (a) illustrates examples
of discovering meaningful directions in the representation
space of RCDM trained on the Celeb-A dataset. To add
an attribute like blonde hair (as shown in the figure 4
(a)) to a reference image, the mean representation of all
faces with blonde hair is computed and added to the ref-
erence image, enabling the desired feature modification.
However, correlations present in the dataset can influence
image generation. For example, adding the bald attribute
may also unintentionally alter the gender to male, as the
dataset contain a higher proportion of bald male images.

spans.content: III. METHOD
Training a Representation Conditioned Diffusion
Model (RCDM) can be broken down into two stages:
First, images are mapped to a lower-dimensional space us-
ing a pre-trained Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) encoder.
Then, a diffusion model is trained while conditioning on
these representations (see Figure 1). In this work, we
adapt the method from [9], which uses the MoCo v3
encoder and the MAGE generator, to generation through
the latent diffusion model (LDM) [10] conditioned on
DINO representations [15]. That is, denoising in the LDM
latent space is guided by the information provided by an
image’s DINO representation. While it is possible to train
a separate representation generator for fully unconditioned
generation, in this work we perform experiments on
representations from a separate test dataset.
A. Data and Training Setup
For the experiments, two datasets were used: (a) LSUN
Churches [16] and (b) Celeb-A [17]. The pre-trained
DINO encoder [15] extracts 768-dimensional represen-
tations from these datasets. For image compression in
LDM, a pre-trained VAE model with KL-divergence loss
[18] was used, and RCDMs were trained for each dataset
using a conditional de-noising U-Net.
IV. PROPERTIES OF THE REPRESENTATION SPACE
In this section, we perform experiments to validate
the effectiveness of using the representation space for
controllable image generation.
A. Perturbation and Interpolation
Given a representation vector C, we sample a noise
vector
\epsilon \sim \mathcal { N } ( 0 , 1 )
and perturb the representation vector
according to
\hat { \mathcal { C } } = \mathcal { C } + \lambda \epsilon .
, where λ controls the perturbation
strength. Figure 3 shows the generated images at different
perturbation strengths for two methods: Diffusion Inver-
sion [5], trained on the STL-10 dataset [19] and RCDM,
trained on LSUN-Churches [16]. At higher noise levels
( \lambda > 0 . 4 )
, Diffusion Inversion exhibits significant quality
degradation, while RCDM modifies image content while
maintaining image quality and consistency.
We can also perform interpolations in the representation
space. Given two representation vectors
\mathcal { C } _ { 1 }
and
\mathcal { C } _ { 2 } .
, a new
vector can be obtained by
\hat { \mathcal { C } } = \alpha \mathcal { C } _ { 1 } + ( 1 - \alpha ) \mathcal { C } _ { 2 }
. The
resulting image generated from
\hat { \mathcal { C } }
will exhibit features of

spans.content: Fig. 3. Images generated by perturbing the embedding vector
\hat { \mathcal { C } }
(from
the reference image) using Gaussian noise with varying strength λ.
both input images, with the influence of each determined
by the interpolation factor α. Figure 2 shows the images
generated at different interpolation strengths for three
methods. In Stable Diffusion [10], two text embeddings
are extracted from separate text prompts using CLIP, and
the generated images represent interpolations between
these embeddings. For Diffusion Inversion and RCDM,
images are produced by interpolating between two learned
embeddings or representations (in the case of RCDM).
The results indicate that RCDM provides a semantically
smoother transition between reference images. In Diffu-
sion Inversion the images become indistinguishable in the
middle, while for Stable Diffusion a sudden transition
between images is observed for α values 0.5-0.6.
B. Semantic Directions
a) Supervised Semantic Directions: In a supervised
setting with access to image annotations, identifying se-
mantic directions in the representation space of RCDM
becomes more direct. Figure 4 (a) illustrates examples
of discovering meaningful directions in the representation
space of RCDM trained on the Celeb-A dataset. To add
an attribute like blonde hair (as shown in the figure 4
(a)) to a reference image, the mean representation of all
faces with blonde hair is computed and added to the ref-
erence image, enabling the desired feature modification.
However, correlations present in the dataset can influence
image generation. For example, adding the bald attribute
may also unintentionally alter the gender to male, as the
dataset contain a higher proportion of bald male images.
Fig. 4. Images generated by identifying semantic directions within the representation space of diffusion models. (a) supervised approach using the
mean representation of a given attribute, (b) unsupervised approach which uses PCA to discover semantic directions.
b) Unsupervised Semantic Directions: Identifying
interpretable directions in the representation space be-
comes challenging in an unsupervised setting. Hark ¨ onen ¨
et al. [4] addressed this issue by applying Principal Com-
ponent Analysis (PCA) to manipulate generated images
along meaningful attributes. We use a similar approach
in the representation-conditioning space, perturbing rep-
resentations according to
\hat { \mathcal { C } } = \mathcal { C } + \alpha V _ { K }
, where
V _ { K }
is
the K:th principal component and α is a scaling factor
controlling the strength of the modification. Figure 4 (b)
presents the results for RCDM trained on Celeb-A with
different components K and a fixed α value of −25.
While these initial results do not show the same amount
of disentanglement and interpretability as compared to
GANs [4], there are variations that suggest it could be
possible to utilize unsupervised discovery of directions
in the representation space. For example, we can see
variations in terms of the size of forehead (K=3), short
hair (K=4), background color (K=6), and long hair (K=9).
V. CONCLUSION
In this work, we have demonstrated that representation-
conditioned diffusion models exhibit promising properties
in the conditioning space, including smoothness and a
certain degree of disentanglement. These characteristics
suggest that representation conditioning is a compelling
approach for improving both the quality and controllabil-
ity of diffusion models. For future work, representations
could be adapted to improve disentanglement, and the
results could be used in applications such as controlled
generation in unconditional diffusion models for image
editing and training data augmentation.
